coredata.dc:title: Large-scale synthesis of dual-emitting-based visualization sensing paper for humidity and ethanol detection
coredata.dc:creator: Wen, Zhuoqi
Song, Shanliang
Wang, Chuanxi
Qu, Fengdong
Thomas, Tiju
Hu, Tantan
Wang, Pei
Yang, Minghui
coredata.dc:description: A novel visual and fluorometric-sensor paper is prepared for the monitoring of atmospheric humidity and ethanol. The paper contains a combination of copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) and carbon dots (CDs), immobilized on a filter paper. This makes it a dual-emission system. The visual sensor shows bright fluorescence and has a diameter of ∼8 cm with uniform and homogeneous surface. The detection mechanism of the sensing paper is based on the fluorescence intensity variation of CuNCs in different environments. Desirable linear relationship is observed between fluorescence intensity ratio (red/blue) and humidity. Visible color transformation of this sensor paper is observed in the humidity range ∼40-80%, implying utility for easy and facile atmospheric humidity determination. Furthermore, due to the water and ethanol have different impacts on the spectrum, this paper-based fluorescent probe shows excellent response to ethanol. Therefore dual-emitting sensing paper, when suitably calibrated, shows promise for visual and fluorometric sensing platforms aimed at humidity or ethanol detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sensing paper
Dual-emission
Visualization
Ethanol
Humidity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Humidity and Alcohol by dual-emitting film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration and the process diagram for the synthesis of CuNCs-CDs dual-emission-based fluorometric film.

(a) PL spectra of CuNCs with increasing ethanol in aqueous solutions from 0 to 40 v/v%; photographs of CuNCs in different ratios of ethanol-water solutions under the sunlight (b) and the UV light (c).
Photographs of filter paper (a), CDs paper-based film (b) and homogeneous CuNCs-CDs fluorescent film (c) under the sunlight (1) and UV light (2).

(a) The emission spectra of CuNCs-CDs dual-emitting film at the optimum excitation wavelength. (b) SEM image of the CuNCs-CDs dual-emitting film; (c) EDAX spectrum of the CuNCs-CDs dual-emitting film; c1 and c2, the EDAX mapping images of Cu and S of the CuNCs-CDs dual-emitting film.

(a) PL spectra of CuNCs-CDs dual-emitting film in different humidity environments from 11.3 to 93.6% at 25 °C. (b) Photographs of CuNCs-CDs dual-emitting film in different humidity environments at 25 °C under the UV light. (c) The relationship between ratiometric fluorescence intensity (I602/I435) and humidity, inset: linear fitting curve of ratiometric fluorescence intensity (I602/I435) and humidity from 40 to 80%. (d) CIE chromaticity diagram of the (x,y) color coordinates of the CuNCs-CDs ratiometric fluorescent film varying with humidity.

(a) Photographs of CuNCs-CDs dual-emitting film in the humidity of 11.3% and 93.6% under the daylight and UV light. (b) CIE coordinates of the CuNCs-CDs dual-emitting film in the humidity of 11.3% and 93.6%.

(a) PL spectra of CuNCs-CDs dual-emitting film with decreasing ethanol in aqueous solutions from 65 to 30 v/v%. (b) Images showing reversibility of CuNCs-CDs dual-emitting film when exposed to air after detections. (c) Photographs of CuNCs-CDs dual-emitting film in different ratios of ethanol-water solutions under the UV light.

head.ce:title: Large-scale synthesis of dual-emitting-based visualization sensing paper for humidity and ethanol detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Science and Engineering Department, Dalian Maritime University, Dalian, 116026, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of New Energy Technology, Ningbo Institute of Industrial Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo, 315201, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Madras, Chennai 600036, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel visual and fluorometric-sensor paper is prepared for the monitoring of atmospheric humidity and ethanol. The paper contains a combination of copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) and carbon dots (CDs), immobilized on a filter paper. This makes it a dual-emission system. The visual sensor shows bright fluorescence and has a diameter of ∼8 cm with uniform and homogeneous surface. The detection mechanism of the sensing paper is based on the fluorescence intensity variation of CuNCs in different environments. Desirable linear relationship is observed between fluorescence intensity ratio (red/blue) and humidity. Visible color transformation of this sensor paper is observed in the humidity range ∼40-80%, implying utility for easy and facile atmospheric humidity determination. Furthermore, due to the water and ethanol have different impacts on the spectrum, this paper-based fluorescent probe shows excellent response to ethanol. Therefore dual-emitting sensing paper, when suitably calibrated, shows promise for visual and fluorometric sensing platforms aimed at humidity or ethanol detection.

ce:section.ce:para: The detection and quantitation of humidity and ethanol are of importance to various fields including environmental monitoring, clinical diagnosis, quality control in biotechnological, food processing, and beverage industries [1–4]. Therefore, its relevant to develop a simple and low cost analytic technique for humidity and ethanol detection. Towards this end, there are research efforts focused on electronic, acoustic and optical sensing mechanisms [5–9]. As an optical sensor, fluorescence sensor is simple, and can in principle be rapid and easy to handle [10,11]. Recent researches have indicated that fluorescence detection not only improves the simplicity and portability of detection, but also has excellent sensitivity and selectivity, enabling it to achieve on-field and real-time detection [12–16]. A notable report here is by Huang et al.; who have prepared fluorescent copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) in organic phase using a facile one-step synthesis [17]. In this sensor, the fluorescence intensity is readily quenched by water. Therefore, it can be used to realize the visual and accurate monitoring of water in organic phases. Likewise, Masaaki et al. have developed an alcohol sensor by mixing the terphenyl-ol (TPhOH) and sodium carbonate [18]. The probe exhibits bright blue ﬂuorescence upon addition of small quantities of ethanol. However, the downside of these designs is that it uses a single emission peak, which can be affected by a range of experimental conditions including excitation intensity, emission collection eﬃciency, probe and analyte concentration [19,20]. In comparison, a dual-emitting fluorescence probe would offer more scope for quantitative determination and distinguishability [21–25].

Generally speaking, ratiometric dual-emitting fluorescence sensors can be fabricated with multiple fluorescent materials or a type of material with multiple fluorescent sources. Wang et al. combined the 4′-N, N-dimethylamino-4-methylacryloylamino chalcone (DMC) and 5, 10, 15, 20-tetra-(4-methylacryloylaminophenyl) porphyrin zinc (TMAPPZn) to prepare a fluorescent ratiometric sensor for water content determination in organic solvents, in which DMC served as a sensitive fluorescent material to respond to water content [26]. Yin et al. synthesized the Ru@MIL−NH2 successfully through a simple one-pot method. This guest-encapsulation metal−organic framework (MOF) has been used for ratiometric ﬂuorescence detection of water in organic solvents [27]. Though dual-emitting ratiometric fluorescent probes have been realized to detect and quantify water and ethanol, there exist problems that require solutions. For instance, since most of these ratiometric probes are used in liquid-state, portability and practical usability could be limited. On the other hand, dual-emitting fluorescent film which has the advantages of low-cost, simple operation, portable feasibility gives a good solution to these problems [28,29]. Similar as pH test paper, the ratiometric fluorescent film can be used in solid-state and increases the user-friendliness substantially [30–32].

In this work, a paper-based ratiometric fluorescent probe for humidity and ethanol is prepared by integrating CuNCs with CDs. This dual-emission visual probe shows bright fluorescence and has a diameter of ∼8 cm with uniform and homogeneous surface. The CuNCs shows water/ethanol dependent fluorescent behavior due to their aggregation induced emission enhancement (AIEE) eﬀ ;ect [33]; while the CDs are used as the reference fluorescent material since the blue fluorescence remains constant with changes in ambiance. With increasing humidity, the color of dual-emitting visual-sensor paper changes from red to blue. Furthermore, visible color transformation and desirable linear relationship is observed between fluorescence intensity ratio (red/blue) and humidity in the humidity range ∼40-80%. On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity of CuNCs will be enhanced in presence of ethanol; the color correlates directly with concentration of ethanol. Therefore, the sensing paper, when suitably calibrated, shows promise for visual and fluorometric sensing platforms aimed at humidity or ethanol detection. Furthermore, this work also provides valuable ideas and experience for the large-scale synthesis uniform fluorescent filter paper.

Copper nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O), isopropanol (IPA), lithium chloride (LiCl), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), potassium carbonate (K2CO3), and potassium iodide (KI) are all purchased from Aladdin Ltd. Reduced glutathione (GSH) is purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Potassium acetate (KAc), magnesium nitrate (Mg(NO3)2), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), potassium nitrate (KNO3), urea and citric acid are obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (China). All of the reagents are used directly without any purification, and deionized water is used throughout all experiments.

The CuNCs are prepared by the following steps. First, 12 ml of GSH solution (0.076 M) is mixed with 3 ml Cu(NO3)2 solution (0.015 M) using magnetic stirring for 10 min. Then NaOH solution (1 M) is added until the pH of solution becomes 6.0. The CuNCs are finally obtained with the assistance of ultrasonic treatment for 15 min. Furthermore, CuNCs are purified with IPA by centrifugation for 5 min at 8000 rpm. The process is repeated three times. The precipitate finally obtained is preserved in a refrigerator.

2 g urea and 1 g citric acid are dissolved in 10 ml water at room temperature, after stirring for 5 min. The mixture is then heated in the Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave for 6 h at 120 °C. The prepared CDs are further purified through dialysis and keep away from light.

CuNCs-CDs dual-emitting film is prepared using a two-step approach. First, the blue fluorescence film of CDs is obtained through immersing the filter paper with CDs solution (3.5 g/l) for 10 min. After drying in air, the blue fluorescence film is dipped into the CuNCs solution (0.0045 M). The dual-emitting visual-sensor film is finally obtained through drying in vacuum oven for 3 h at 45 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Humidity and Alcohol by dual-emitting film
ce:section.ce:para:
Different saturated salt solutions (LiCl, KAc, MgCl2, K2CO3, Mg(NO3)2, KI, NaCl, KCl, KNO3) are prepared and stored at 25 °C to provide a series of gradient humidity (11.3%, 22.5%, 32.8%, 43.2%, 54.4%, 68.9%, 75.3%, 84.3%, 93.6%) respectively. The dual-emission visual film is sealed in different humidity atmospheres for 10 min at 25 °C. Subsequently the fluorescence intensity was detected by fluorescence spectrophotometer. The different alcohol contents (30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 55, 60 and 65%) are prepared by adjusting the ratio of ethanol and water. The fluorescence intensity of dual-emitting film is measured immediately after the mixtures are dripped on the film. Furthermore, in order to guarantee the accuracy of the experimental results, all of the relevant measurements were repeated three times and the error is calculated according to the following formula: error = max
{
X 1
-
X ¯
,
X 2
-
X ¯
,
X 3
-
X ¯
}.

Fluorescence spectra are detected on the Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluoromax 4C–L (France) spectrophotometer at the optimal emission wavelengths, UV–vis absorption spectra are obtained on the UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi U–3900 Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images are conducted on Tecnai GI F20 U–TWIN with the accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE–SEM) images are performed by the Hitachi S4800\EDS.

Carbon dots (CDs) are synthesized and found to be consistent in their behavior with previous reports [34,35]. The UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CDs are shown in Fig. S1a. A minor absorbance band at ∼280 nm and an obviously absorption band centered at ∼354 nm can be observed. The latter is usually assigned to the n–π* transition of conjugated CO and CN, which is basically corresponding to the optimal excitation peak of 370 nm [36]. With this optimal excitation, the resultant CDs solution emit bright blue fluorescence with optimal emission at 445 nm (inset of Fig. S1a). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. S1b) demonstrates that the CDs have a monomodal and normal size distribution, centered at ∼3.0 nm.

The copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) are prepared by a one-pot sonochemical route [37]. As depicted in Fig. S2a, the UV–vis absorption spectrum shows a faint absorption at ∼260 nm rather than the characteristic absorbance band of Cu nanoparticles at 507 nm. This indicates the formation of CuNCs [38]. Furthermore, the asprepared CuNCs show transparent yellow color in daylight, while they emit red fluorescence under UV lamp (inset of Fig. S2a). The emission peak at ∼622 nm is in the range of 500–800 nm and the optimal excitation wavelength is found to be 425 nm. Monodispersed CuNCs can be clearly observed in the TEM image (Fig. S2b), which indicates that the red fluorescence comes from the nanoparticles having sizes in the 0.5–2.5 nm range.

To confirm the aggregation induced emission enhancement (AIEE) effect of CuNCs, the fluorescence of the CuNCs in water/ethanol mixture with different volumetric fractions of ethanol is detected. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the PL intensity at 620 nm is enhanced gradually with increasing concentrations of ethanol. Correspondingly, the transparent solution of CuNCs becomes turbid in daylight. The red fluorescence of CuNCs become strong under the UV lamp (as shown in Fig. 1b and c). This phenomenon can be attributed to the different solubilities of CuNCs in water and ethanol. Considering poor solubility of the GSH in ethanol; the stretch of GSH molecules and hence intramolecular motions such as molecular vibrational, rotational, and torsional movements are restricted. Hence the nonradiative path is blocked and the radiative decay is activated; this is concomitant with enhanced emission efficiency observed [39]. With this process, the faint red fluorescence becomes intense. Furthermore, this behavior can be reversed by adding water into the turbid system. As the proportion of water increases gradually, the mixture reforms into a clear solution and bright red fluorescence fades away.

The CuNCs-CDs film is fabricated through two steps. As depicted in Scheme 1 , the blue fluorescence film of CDs is obtained through infiltrating the filter paper with CDs solution for 10 min and drying in air firstly. The second step is dipping the blue fluorescence film into the CuNCs solution and the dual-emitting visual-sensor film is finally obtained through drying in vacuum oven for 3 h at 45 °C. As shown in Fig. 2 , from the filter paper, no luminescence is observed under UV lamp (Fig. 2a2). This means the original film has no luminescence property of its own. After impregnation, bright blue fluorescence is observed, indicating that the CDs have attached on to the filter film successfully (Fig. 2b2 and Fig. S3). Subsequently, the CDs film is immersed into the CuNCs solution and dried in vacuum oven. By comparing the CDs film (Fig. 2b2) and CuNCs-CDs film (Fig. 2c2), red fluorescence is found, ascribed to the CuNCs. In fact, electrostatic interactions between CuNCs and CDs is responsible for the efficient immobilization of CuNCs. Even though the diameter of the film reaches 8 cm, the homogenous fluorescence is still observed, clearly indicating the sufficiently uniform distribution of CuNCs and CDs on the paper. Meanwhile, it is worth to mention that the red emission peaks of the CuNCs-CDs film are blue-shifted about 22 nm compared with the CuNCs solutions (Fig. 3 a). This phenomenon is in fact known from previous reports [40,41]. Considering the predominance of the inter-Cu interactions over the intra-Cu interactions in the clusters with the aggregation, resulting in an increase of average Cu(I)···Cu(I) distance, these films have the high concentration of fluorescent particles and should emit at shorter wavelengths.

The emission spectra of CuNCs-CDs film is shown in Fig. 3a. With an excitation wavelength of ∼350 nm, two emission peaks at 437 nm and 600 nm are observed, corresponding to the blue and red fluorescence of CDs and CuNCs respectively. Energy-dispersive analysis of X–rays (EDAX) indicates the existence of copper and sulfur on the film (Fig. 3c), as expected. The field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE–SEM) images of the CuNCs-CDs film are shown in Fig. 3b; the dense distribution of the cooper and sulfur along the surface of the filter paper shows homogeneity down to the micro level. Furthermore, the denser distribution of sulfur compared with copper reflects the fact that the CuNCs are stabilized by GSH. Thus, the method is relevant for dual-emitting fluorescent films with large areas.

A series of humidity environments are provided by sealing different saturated salt solutions in jars to explore the responsiveness of fluorescent probes to humidity [8]. During the detection, the film is placed in sealed jars, after stabilizing it for 10 min to reduce any change in humidity levels caused by air flow. The PL intensity of the film is finally recorded. Under the UV lamp, the CDs-based fluorescent films presented stable blue fluorescence, and the PL intensity shows no change with increasing humidity. This further implies that the blue fluorescent CDs can be used as the reference material in this novel dual-emitting fluorescent films (Fig. S4).

The red fluorescence of CuNCs in solid state would become weak with increasing humidity due to their dispersion. Thus, as-prepared dual-emitting film can be used as sensing paper for humidity. As shown in Fig. 4 a and b, with gradual increase in the humidity levels, the emission peak provided by CuNCs at ∼600 nm wears off (i.e diminishes), while the blue peak stays the same. The color of the paper-based ratiometric fluorescent probe shows a change from red to blue correspondingly. The red peak also shows a red-shift about 20 nm with the change of humidity; this is in fact consistent with what is known from previous reports on CuNCs [41]. As the vapour gradually wets the film, CuNCs assembled on the film are dispersed. Considering the fact that the denser aggregates emit at shorter wavelengths, the dispersed CuNCs would have a red-shift.

Fig. 4c shows a linear fit between PL intensity ratio (I602/I435) and humidity levels (40%–80%) of the paper-based ratiometric fluorescent probe. The linear regression equation can be expressed as y= -0.00957x+0.94439 where R2 = 0.99778. Combining the color change of the probe under the UV lamp and considering the fact that the accuracy of the probe based on R2 is close to 1; the results obtained above indicates that the film can be used as a visual probe to monitor the air humidity in living environment.

The CIE (Commission International edel’Eclairage 1931) diagram is used to show the variation in the fluorescence color from the initial red (0.325,0.203) to blue (0.267,0.161). (Fig. 4d). As shown in Fig. 5 , different saturated salt solutions (LiCl and KNO3) are sealed in bottles to simulate the atmospheric humidity respectively (11.3% and 93.6%). With the strips of fluorescent probe put into the system and stabilized for 10 min, the colors of the probe are found to be markedly different. Because of the AIE effect of the CuNCs, the probe in dry environment emits red fluorescence under UV light; while the other one emits blue. These results clearly demonstrate CuNCs-CDs dual-emitting film is a novel visual-sensor paper for humidity.

The mechanism for humidity detection is based on the AIEE effect of CuNCs. Different from traditional fluorescent dyes, fluorescence of CuNCs can be enhanced in the aggregated state significantly. Because of the excellent solubility in water, the fluorescence intensity of CuNCs shows a regular trend with the adjustment of the proportion of water. As for paper-based ratiometric fluorescent probe, the red fluorescence provided by CuNCs varies with humidity due to the AIEE property, while the blue one provided by the carbon dots is almost unaffected (Fig. S4). Finally, dual-emission fluorescent filter paper enables visual colorimetric detection of humidity.

Considering that ethanol can aggregate CuNCs but with little effect on CDs (Fig. S5), the dual-emitting sensor film is expected to detect the alcohol content in liquor. As depicted in Fig. 6 a, with decreasing volumetric fractions of ethanol, the emission peak at 612 nm become weak while the emission peak at 445 nm remains constant. This results in red fluorescence of the probe turning to blue eventually (Fig. 6b). Also, it is important to mention here that the quenched paper-based ratiometric fluorescent probe recovers on exposure to air. As shown in Fig. S6, the red fluorescence of the probe is quenched immediately when solution of low alcohol is used. About 5 min later, the red fluorescence begins to appear on the edge of the probe and spreads over the entire surface as time goes on. This process takes about 15 min. This phenomenon is related to the volatility of ethanol. As the probe gets exposed to air, ethanol rapidly evaporates with water and the dispersed CuNCs are reassembled again, which leads to the color of the probe from blue to red. Furthermore, this “quench-recovery” process can be repeated multiple cycles. As depicted in Fig. 6b, the fluorescence intensity of the probe has hardly decreased in three repetitions compared with the original one. This indicates that the paper-based ratiometric fluorescent probe can be used repeatedly in actual detections.

A ratiometric and visual-sensor paper is developed by immobilizing CuNCs (red fluorescence) and CDs (blue fluorescence). Characterization indicates that the approach used here results in fairly homogenous distribution of CuNCs and CDs over a large length scale (∼8 cm). The red color fluorescence of CuNCs change in response to humidity and ethanol levels, due to the aggregation of the fluorescent nanoparticles. The observed color of CuNCs-CDs dual-emitting film correlates directly with the water content (40–80% RH). Furthermore, this ratiometric fluorescence sensors can displayed/measured the ratio of the combination of water and ethanol when one of the parameters is known. Compared with the previous works, this probe not only improves the portability and economy of visual detection by overcoming the shortcomings of single emission detection and solution system detection, but also achieves the detection of humidity and ethanol simultaneously. We anticipate that this report will open avenues for exploring means of (i) making large-scale sensor-papers for other applications, and (ii) achieving enhanced sensitivities and selectivities for the paper reported here.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. LY18E030010, and National Key Research and Development Program of China through grant 2016YFB0101200, 2016YFB0101205. Dr. C. Wang would like to thank for open project of state key laboratory of supramolecular structure and materials (sklssm201819). Dr. M. Yang would like to thank for the National "Thousand Youth Talents" program of China and Ningbo 3315 program.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.11.041.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for the detection of humidity or water content
Graphene oxide in lossy mode resonance-based optical fiber sensors for ethanol detection
A rapid method for simultaneously determining ethanol and methanol content in wines by full evaporation headspace gas chromatography
Effective visualization assay for alcohol content sensing and methanol differentiation with solvent stimuli-responsive supramolecular ionic materials
Dual-mode humidity detection using a lanthanide-based metal–organic framework: towards multifunctional humidity sensors
Thermochemical humidity detection in harsh or non-steady environments
Detection of very low humidity using polyelectrolyte/graphene bilayer humidity sensors
Novel high-performance self-powered humidity detection enabled by triboelectric effect
Recent trends of ceramic humidity sensors development: a review
A new fluorescent probe for gasotransmitter H2S: high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, and a significant fluorescence off–on response
Specific enzymatic synthesis of 2,3-diaminophenazine and copper nanoclusters used for dual-emission ratiometric and naked-eye visual fluorescence sensing of choline
A selective and sensitive optical sensor for dissolved ammonia detection via agglomeration of fluorescent Ag nanoclusters and temperature gradient headspace single drop microextraction
Dual-colored carbon dot ratiometric fluorescent test paper based on a specific spectral energy transfer for semiquantitative assay of copper ions

Melamine-induced decomposition and anti-FRET effect from a self-assembled complex of rhodamine 6G and DNA-stabilized silver nanoclusters used for dual-emitting ratiometric and naked-eye-visible fluorescence detection
An anthraquinone-based highly selective colorimetric and fluorometric sensor for sequential detection of Cu2+ and S2− with intracellular application
Reversible photochromic system based on rhodamine B salicylaldehyde hydrazone metal complex
Luminescent nanoswitch based on organic-phase copper nanoclusters for sensitive detection of trace amount of water in organic solvents
Detection of ethanol in alcoholic beverages or vapor phase using fluorescent molecules embedded in a nanofibrous polymer
Ratiometric fluorescence, solution-phase and filter-paper visualization detection of ciprofloxacin based on dual-emitting carbon dot/silicon dot hybrids
Boric-acid-functional lanthanide metal-organic frameworks for selective ratiometric fluorescence detection of fluoride ions
Ratiometric fluorescent detection of Cu2+ with carbon dots chelated Eu-based metal-organic frameworks
A strap strategy for construction of an excited-state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) system with dual fluorescence
Solid-state carbon dots with red fluorescence and efficient construction of dual-fluorescence morphologies
A highly sensitive and selective cyanide detection using a gold nanoparticle-based dual fluorescence–colorimetric sensor with a wide concentration range
Ratiometric fluorescence and visual imaging detection of dopamine based on carbon dots/copper nanoclusters dual-emitting nanohybrids
A fluorescent ratiometric sensor based on covalent immobilization of chalcone derivative and porphyrin zinc for detecting water content in organic solvents
Ratiometric fluorescence sensing and real-time detection of water in organic solvents with one-pot synthesis of Ru@MIL-101(Al)-NH2
Gold-cluster-based dual-emission nanocomposite film as ratiometric fluorescent sensing paper for specific metal ion
Size-tunable copper nanocluster aggregates and their application in hydrogen sulfide sensing on paper-based devices
Laminated copper nanocluster incorporated antioxidative paper device with RGB system-assisted signal improvement
Comparative performance evaluation of carbon dot-based paper immunoassay on Whatman filter paper and nitrocellulose paper in the detection of HIV infection
Highly selective, rapid-functioning and sensitive fluorescent test paper based on graphene quantum dots for on-line detection of metal ions
Contribution of metal defects in the assembly induced emission of Cu nanoclusters
Synthesis of carbon dots with multiple color emission by controlled graphitization and surface functionalization
Full-color inorganic carbon dot phosphors for white-light-emitting diodes
Rapid sonochemical synthesis of luminescent and paramagnetic copper nanoclusters for bimodal bioimaging
Facile sonochemical synthesis of pH-responsive copper nanoclusters for selective and sensitive detection of Pb2+ in living cells
Cu nanoclusters with aggregation induced emission enhancement
Luminescent metal nanoclusters with aggregation-induced emission
All-copper nanocluster based down-conversion white light-emitting devices

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zhuoqi Wen is currently a master student under the supervision of Prof. Pei Wang at Materials Science and Engineering Department, Dalian Maritime University. His research interests are the synthesis and application of dual-emission nanocomposite materials.

Shanliang Song is currently a pH.D. student under the supervision of Prof. Quan Lin at State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, Jilin University. His research interests are the synthesis and characterization of noble-metal nanoclusters in water phase and dual-emission nanocomposite materials.

Chuanxi Wang is associate professor of Materials Chemistry at Ningbo Institute of Industrial Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences. He received his pH.D. from Jilin University in 2013. From 2013.7–2016.12 he worked as associate professor in Jiangnan University. His research interests include the development of inorganic semiconductor nanostructures and their applications in biomedical and optoelectronic.

Tantan Hu is currently studying for a master’s degree at Jiangnan University, China and majored in analytical chemistry. Her current interest is the synthesis and characterization of the carbon-based fluorescentl materials and optoelectronic devices.

Fengdong Qu received his B.S. and M.S. degrees from Jilin University in 2012 and 2015, respectively. Now, he is a research assistant in Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences. His present works mainly focus on synthesis of solid state functional materials, gas sensors, and photoelectric devices.

Tiju Thomas is an assistant professor at the Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering (Indian Institute of Technology Madras, IITM) in Chennai, India. His research group focuses on developing compositionally complex oxides, oxynitrides and nitrides, and nanometals for achieving engineering ends.

Pei Wang is associate professor of Materials Science and Engineering Department, Dalian Maritime University. He received his pH.D. from Dalian University of Technology in 2005. From 2005–2018 he worked as associate professor in Dalian Maritime University. His research interests include the synthesis and modification of nano-hydrogel materials, preparation of high value-added coatings, construction of biological anti-fouling coating systems, and development of environmentally friendly materials.

Minghui Yang is a professor of Materials Chemistry at Ningbo Institute of Industrial Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences. He is a graduate of the University of Liverpool and completed his pH.D. in Materials Chemistry under the supervision of Professor J. Paul Attfield and Professor Amparo Fuertes at University of Edinburgh in 2010. This was followed by a postdoctoral appointment in the Department of Chemistry and Chemical Biology at Cornell University, working on nano-structured functional materials for renewable energy and environmental applications with Professor Francis J. DiSalvo at Energy Materials Center at Cornell. His research interests include synthesis of metal (oxy)nitrides and application in photocatalysis, fuel cells, Li-S battery and gas sensor.
